days.title: Relativsätze im Nominativ
days.objective: Describe people and things with relative clauses in which the relative pronoun is the subject of its own clause.
days.paretoFocus: Relative clauses join two short sentences into one, which is the fastest way to stop sounding like a beginner in both speech and writing.
drills: Write five sentences about people in your building, each with a relative clause whose pronoun is the subject.
Take four short sentence pairs you already know and join each pair into one sentence with a relative clause, checking the comma every time.
Look around the room and describe four objects, saying what each one does inside the relative clause.

items: Where does the verb go in a German relative clause, and what punctuation always separates it?
Which noun decides the gender and number of the relative pronoun, and what decides its case?
Say the nominative relative pronouns for masculine, feminine, neuter and plural.

activities.title: Der, die, das als Relativpronomen
activities.intro: A relative clause adds information about a noun you have just mentioned. The relative pronoun takes its gender and number from the noun it refers back to, but its case from its own job inside the relative clause. Here the pronoun is the subject, so it is nominative: der, die, das, and die in the plural. A relative clause is a subordinate clause, so the verb goes to the end, and German always separates it with a comma, even where English uses none.

items.hr: Das ist der Mann, der neben mir wohnt.
items.en: That is the man who lives next to me.
items.note: der Mann is masculine, and the pronoun is the subject of wohnt, so der.

items.hr: Ich kenne die Frau, die den Kurs leitet.
items.en: I know the woman who runs the course.
items.note: die Frau is feminine singular and the subject of leitet.

items.hr: Wir suchen ein Zimmer, das ruhig und hell ist.
items.en: We are looking for a room that is quiet and bright.
items.note: das Zimmer is neuter, so das; the verb ist stands at the end.

items.hr: Die Kollegen, die neu angefangen haben, sitzen im ersten Stock.
items.en: The colleagues who have just started sit on the first floor.
items.note: A relative clause inside the sentence needs a comma on both sides.

items.hr: Kennst du den Zug, der um acht Uhr abfährt?
items.en: Do you know the train that leaves at eight?
items.note: den Zug is accusative in the main clause, but der in the relative clause because it is the subject there.

activities.title: Wer macht was?
activities.intro: Choose and complete the relative pronoun when it is the subject of its clause.

questions.prompt: Das ist die Kollegin, ___ das Projekt leitet.
questions.explanation: die Kollegin is feminine singular, and the pronoun is the subject of leitet, so you need the feminine nominative form die.

questions.prompt: Ich habe einen Nachbarn, ___ jeden Morgen joggt.
questions.explanation: The pronoun refers back to der Nachbar, masculine, and is the subject of joggt, so nominative der. The accusative einen Nachbarn in the main clause has no effect on it.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct relative pronoun: Wir wohnen in einem Haus, ___ über hundert Jahre alt ist.
questions.explanation: das Haus is neuter and the pronoun is the subject of ist, so the neuter nominative form is needed.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct relative pronoun: Meine Freunde, ___ mich besucht haben, kommen aus Hamburg.
questions.explanation: The pronoun refers back to a plural noun and is the subject of besucht haben, so the plural nominative form is used for all genders.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am looking for a bag that is not too heavy.'
questions.explanation: die Tasche is feminine and the pronoun is the subject of ist, so die, and the verb ist closes the relative clause.

activities.title: Wer ist das auf dem Foto?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend shows you photos and you ask about the people, using relative clauses whose pronoun is the subject.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schau mal, das ist der Kollege, der mir beim Umzug geholfen hat.
lines.en: Look, that is the colleague who helped me with my move.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Und wer ist die Frau, die neben ihm steht?
lines.en: And who is the woman who is standing next to him?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das ist seine Schwester, die in Leipzig Medizin studiert.
lines.en: That is his sister, who studies medicine in Leipzig.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ist das der Hund, der immer so laut bellt?
lines.en: Is that the dog that always barks so loudly?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Genau. Und das Kind, das vorne sitzt, ist mein Neffe.
lines.en: Exactly. And the child who is sitting at the front is my nephew.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ihr habt eine Familie, die wirklich groß ist.
lines.en: You have a family that is really big.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, und wir haben Nachbarn, die fast immer mitfeiern.
lines.en: Yes, and we have neighbours who nearly always celebrate with us.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das klingt nach Leuten, die gern zusammen sind.
lines.en: That sounds like people who like being together.

days.title: Relativsätze im Akkusativ
days.objective: Use relative clauses in which the relative pronoun is the direct object, choosing den for masculine nouns.
days.paretoFocus: Talking about things you bought, saw, read or lost almost always needs an object relative clause, and German never drops the pronoun the way English drops 'that'.
drills: Name five things you own and add a relative clause saying where or when you got each one.
Write four sentences about films, books or songs, each with the relative pronoun as the object.
Say aloud three sentences with a masculine noun and check that you used den, not der.

items: Which relative pronoun changes its form in the accusative, and which three stay the same?
Why is it den Film, den ich gesehen habe, and not der Film, der ich gesehen habe?
What does German always keep that English can leave out in this kind of clause?

activities.title: Den Film, den ich gesehen habe
activities.intro: When the relative pronoun is the direct object of its own clause, it goes into the accusative. Only the masculine form changes, to den; die, das and the plural die look exactly as they do in the nominative. Gender and number still come from the noun outside the clause, and the case still comes from the job inside it.

items.hr: Das ist der Film, den ich gestern gesehen habe.
items.en: That is the film that I saw yesterday.
items.note: der Film is masculine and the object of gesehen habe, so den.

items.hr: Die Tasche, die ich gekauft habe, war ziemlich teuer.
items.en: The bag that I bought was quite expensive.
items.note: The feminine accusative looks like the nominative: die.

items.hr: Ich habe das Buch, das du mir empfohlen hast, endlich gelesen.
items.en: I have finally read the book that you recommended to me.
items.note: das Buch is neuter and the object of empfohlen hast.

items.hr: Die Leute, die wir eingeladen haben, kommen um sieben.
items.en: The people we invited are coming at seven.
items.note: English can drop 'that' here, German never drops the relative pronoun.

items.hr: Wo ist der Schlüssel, den du in die Schublade gelegt hast?
items.en: Where is the key that you put in the drawer?
items.note: der Schlüssel is the subject of the main clause but the object inside the relative clause, so den.

activities.title: Der oder den?
activities.intro: Practise choosing the relative pronoun when it is the direct object of its clause.

questions.prompt: Das ist der Kuchen, ___ meine Mutter gebacken hat.
questions.explanation: der Kuchen is masculine and it is the direct object of gebacken hat, so the accusative form den is required.

questions.prompt: Wie heißt das Lied, ___ du im Auto gespielt hast?
questions.explanation: das Lied is neuter, and the neuter accusative relative pronoun has the same form as the nominative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct relative pronoun: Der Mantel, ___ ich anprobiert habe, war leider zu klein.
questions.explanation: The noun is masculine and the pronoun is the object of anprobiert habe, so the masculine accusative form is used.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct relative pronoun: Ich habe meine Brille, ___ ich überall gesucht habe, endlich gefunden.
questions.explanation: die Brille is feminine and the object of gesucht habe; the feminine accusative form is identical to the nominative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The letter that I wrote is still on the table.'
questions.explanation: der Brief is masculine and the object inside the clause, so den, and the relative clause is closed by a second comma before the main verb liegt.

activities.title: Im Secondhandladen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend talk about clothes you bought and clothes you are still looking for, using object relative clauses.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ist das die Jacke, die du letzte Woche bestellt hast?
lines.en: Is that the jacket that you ordered last week?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, das ist eine andere. Die Jacke, die ich bestellt habe, war zu eng.
lines.en: No, that is a different one. The jacket I ordered was too tight.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und der Pullover, den du gerade anschaust?
lines.en: And the jumper you are looking at right now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Den finde ich schön, aber ich habe schon einen, den ich fast nie trage.
lines.en: I like that one, but I already have one that I hardly ever wear.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dann nimm lieber die Schuhe, die du vorhin gesehen hast.
lines.en: Then take the shoes you saw earlier instead.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Schuhe, die ich meine, gibt es leider nicht in meiner Größe.
lines.en: The shoes I mean are unfortunately not available in my size.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schade. Und das Hemd, das ich dir vorhin gezeigt habe?
lines.en: What a pity. And the shirt I showed you earlier?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das nehme ich. Es ist genau das Teil, das mir noch gefehlt hat.
lines.en: I will take that one. It is exactly the item I was still missing.

days.title: Relativsätze im Dativ
days.objective: Use dative relative pronouns, including the plural form denen, after dative verbs and for indirect objects.
days.paretoFocus: Verbs like helfen, gehören, danken and geben are everywhere in daily life, so the dative relative pronoun is what lets you talk about the people involved in them.
drills: List four dative verbs you know and write one relative clause with each.
Write three sentences about things that belong to other people, using gehören inside the relative clause.
Say five plural sentences out loud and check that you used denen and not den.

items: Name the four dative relative pronouns, singular and plural.
Which plural form is the one learners most often get wrong, and what do they say instead?
Name three verbs that force the dative on the relative pronoun.

activities.title: Dem, der, denen
activities.intro: When the relative pronoun is the indirect object of its clause, or when it follows a verb that governs the dative such as helfen, gehören, danken or gefallen, it takes the dative: dem, der, dem, and denen in the plural. The plural denen is the form learners forget most often. Gender and number still come from the noun outside, the case from the job inside.

items.hr: Das ist der Lehrer, dem ich alles verdanke.
items.en: That is the teacher to whom I owe everything.
items.note: verdanken takes a dative object, so dem.

items.hr: Die Frau, der ich geholfen habe, hat sich sehr gefreut.
items.en: The woman I helped was very pleased.
items.note: helfen takes the dative, and die Frau is feminine, so der.

items.hr: Das Kind, dem wir das Fahrrad geschenkt haben, wohnt nebenan.
items.en: The child we gave the bike to lives next door.
items.note: Here the pronoun is the indirect object: dem Kind.

items.hr: Die Kollegen, denen ich geschrieben habe, antworten nie.
items.en: The colleagues I wrote to never answer.
items.note: The plural dative is denen, never den.

items.hr: Wie heißt der Freund, dem das Auto gehört?
items.en: What is the name of the friend the car belongs to?
items.note: gehören always takes a dative object.

activities.title: Wem gehört das?
activities.intro: Practise dative relative pronouns after dative verbs and with indirect objects.

questions.prompt: Das ist die Nachbarin, ___ ich beim Einkaufen helfe.
questions.explanation: helfen governs the dative, and die Nachbarin is feminine, so the feminine dative form der is needed.

questions.prompt: Die Studenten, ___ ich das Buch geliehen habe, geben es nie zurück.
questions.explanation: The pronoun is the indirect object of geliehen habe and refers to a plural noun, and the plural dative relative pronoun is denen, not den.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct relative pronoun: Der Junge, ___ wir gestern geholfen haben, wohnt im Nachbarhaus.
questions.explanation: der Junge is masculine and helfen governs the dative, so the masculine dative form is required.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct relative pronoun: Wie heißt die Firma, ___ das Gebäude am Marktplatz gehört?
questions.explanation: gehören governs the dative and die Firma is feminine, so the feminine dative form is used.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The neighbours I lent the drill to have moved away.'
questions.explanation: The pronoun refers to a plural noun and is the indirect object of geliehen habe, so denen, with the verb at the end of the clause.

activities.title: Wem sollen wir danken?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. After a party you and a friend work out who still needs a thank-you message, using dative relative clauses.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wir sollten den Leuten schreiben, denen wir noch nicht gedankt haben.
lines.en: We should write to the people we have not thanked yet.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Der Nachbar, dem die Stühle gehören, bekommt auf jeden Fall eine Nachricht.
lines.en: The neighbour the chairs belong to will definitely get a message.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Kollegin, der du das Rezept versprochen hast?
lines.en: And the colleague you promised the recipe to?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Stimmt. Ihr schicke ich es heute Abend.
lines.en: True. I will send it to her this evening.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was ist mit dem Studenten, dem wir die Musik verdanken?
lines.en: What about the student we have to thank for the music?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Den rufe ich an. Er ist der Einzige, dem ich das persönlich sagen möchte.
lines.en: I will call him. He is the only one I want to tell that in person.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Kinder, denen wir den Kuchen mitgegeben haben?
lines.en: And the children we sent the cake home with?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Familie, der ich schon geschrieben habe, hat sich gestern bedankt.
lines.en: The family I have already written to said thank you yesterday.

days.title: Relativsätze mit Präposition
days.objective: Build relative clauses with a preposition, putting the preposition before the pronoun and letting it decide the case.
days.paretoFocus: Almost every description of your work, your friends and your plans needs a preposition inside the relative clause, and this is the pattern where English word order misleads you most.
drills: Write four sentences about people in your life using mit dem, mit der or mit denen.
List three verbs that take a fixed preposition and build one relative clause with each.
Take three English sentences that end in a preposition and rebuild them in German with the preposition at the front of the clause.

items: Where does the preposition stand in a German relative clause, and what does it decide?
Why is 'der Kollege, den ich arbeite mit' impossible?
Give the plural form used after mit, and say why it is not den.

activities.title: Der Kollege, mit dem ich arbeite
activities.intro: When the relative clause needs a preposition, the preposition stands directly before the relative pronoun, and the preposition alone fixes the case. English can leave the preposition stranded at the end of the clause, and German never can: it is der Kollege, mit dem ich arbeite, and never der Kollege, den ich arbeite mit. The gender and number still come from the noun outside the clause.

items.hr: Das ist der Kollege, mit dem ich jeden Tag arbeite.
items.en: That is the colleague I work with every day.
items.note: mit governs the dative, so mit dem.

items.hr: Die Firma, bei der ich arbeite, sitzt in Köln.
items.en: The company I work for is based in Cologne.
items.note: bei governs the dative, and die Firma is feminine, so bei der.

items.hr: Der Termin, auf den ich warte, ist noch nicht bestätigt.
items.en: The appointment I am waiting for has not been confirmed yet.
items.note: warten auf takes the accusative, so auf den.

items.hr: Das Thema, über das wir gesprochen haben, war ziemlich heikel.
items.en: The topic we talked about was quite delicate.
items.note: sprechen über takes the accusative, so über das.

items.hr: Die Freunde, mit denen ich in Urlaub fahre, wohnen in Bremen.
items.en: The friends I go on holiday with live in Bremen.
items.note: The plural dative after mit is denen.

activities.title: Die Präposition kommt zuerst
activities.intro: Practise placing the preposition in front of the pronoun and choosing the case it governs.

questions.prompt: Das ist der Verein, in ___ ich seit zehn Jahren bin.
questions.explanation: in with a static position answers wo and therefore takes the dative, and der Verein is masculine, so in dem.

questions.prompt: Der Brief, auf ___ ich seit Wochen warte, ist endlich da.
questions.explanation: warten auf always takes the accusative, and der Brief is masculine, so auf den.

questions.prompt: Complete with the two missing words: Das ist die Kollegin, ___ ___ ich das Büro teile.
questions.explanation: teilen mit governs the dative and die Kollegin is feminine, so mit der, with the preposition standing before the pronoun.

questions.prompt: Complete with the two words that are missing: Das Haus, ___ ___ wir früher gewohnt haben, wurde verkauft.
questions.explanation: wohnen answers wo, so in takes the dative, and das Haus is neuter, giving in dem.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The train I am waiting for is twenty minutes late.'
questions.explanation: warten auf takes the accusative, so the masculine pronoun is den, and the preposition must come first, never at the end.

activities.title: Die Halle, in der wir trainieren
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about your sports club and you answer with prepositions in front of the relative pronoun.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie heißt der Trainer, mit dem ihr jetzt arbeitet?
lines.en: What is the name of the coach you work with now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er heißt Sedlmeier. Das ist der Mann, über den ich dir schon erzählt habe.
lines.en: His name is Sedlmeier. That is the man I already told you about.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Halle, in der ihr trainiert, ist die neu?
lines.en: And the hall you train in, is it new?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sie gehört der Stadt, von der wir sie günstig mieten.
lines.en: It belongs to the city, which we rent it from cheaply.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gibt es ein Turnier, auf das ihr euch gerade vorbereitet?
lines.en: Is there a tournament you are preparing for at the moment?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Im Mai. Das ist der Termin, auf den sich alle freuen.
lines.en: In May. That is the date everyone is looking forward to.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Leute, mit denen du am liebsten spielst?
lines.en: And the people you most like playing with?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das sind die Freunde, mit denen ich schon in der Schule gespielt habe.
lines.en: Those are the friends I already played with at school.

days.title: Der Genitiv
days.objective: Form and understand the genitive for possession, and recognise when spoken German replaces it with von plus the dative.
days.paretoFocus: The genitive is the fourth case you have been missing, and written German expects it everywhere that English uses an apostrophe or the word 'of'.
drills: Write six phrases with the genitive, two masculine, two feminine and two plural.
Take three of them and say them again with von plus the dative, the way people speak.
Find four things in the room and write who each one belongs to, using the genitive.

items: Which question word asks for the genitive?
Which two genders add an ending to the noun itself, and what is that ending?
Which construction replaces the genitive in conversation, and where is that replacement not appropriate?

activities.title: Das Auto meines Bruders
activities.intro: The genitive shows possession and answers the question wessen. Masculine and neuter nouns take des or meines and add -s or -es to the noun itself, while feminine and plural nouns take der or meiner and leave the noun unchanged. In everyday speech many people replace the genitive with von plus the dative, das Auto von meinem Bruder, but written German still expects the genitive, and the written part of an exam is marked on exactly that.

items.hr: Das ist das Auto meines Bruders.
items.en: That is my brother's car.
items.note: Masculine: meines plus -s on der Bruder.

items.hr: Die Tür der Wohnung ist kaputt.
items.en: The door of the flat is broken.
items.note: Feminine: der, and die Wohnung takes no ending.

items.hr: Die Farbe des Hemdes gefällt mir nicht.
items.en: I do not like the colour of the shirt.
items.note: Short neuter nouns often take -es: des Hemdes.

items.hr: Die Namen der Kinder stehen schon auf der Liste.
items.en: The children's names are already on the list.
items.note: The plural genitive article is der for all genders.

items.hr: Im Gespräch sagt man oft: das Auto von meinem Bruder.
items.en: In conversation people often say: das Auto von meinem Bruder.
items.note: von plus dative replaces the genitive in speech, but not in writing.

activities.title: Wessen?
activities.intro: Practise the genitive forms and tell the written standard apart from the spoken alternative.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is the standard written form?
options: Das ist das Haus von meinen Eltern.
Das ist das Haus meiner Eltern.
Das ist das Haus meine Eltern.
Das ist das Haus der Eltern meine.
questions.answer: Das ist das Haus meiner Eltern.
questions.explanation: The plural genitive is meiner Eltern. The version with von plus the dative is perfectly common in speech, but writing expects the genitive.

questions.prompt: Der Titel ___ Buches ist mir leider entfallen.
questions.explanation: das Buch is neuter, so the genitive article is des and the noun itself adds -es.

questions.prompt: Put the words in brackets into the genitive: Das Fahrrad ___ steht noch im Hof. (mein Sohn)
questions.explanation: Masculine genitive: the article takes -es and the noun adds -es or -s.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct genitive article: Die Adresse ___ Firma steht im Vertrag.
questions.explanation: die Firma is feminine, so the genitive article is the same form as the feminine dative and the noun keeps its shape.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The colour of the new car is really nice.'
questions.explanation: das Auto is neuter, so des neuen Autos, with the adjective taking -en and the noun taking -s.

activities.title: Wessen Sachen sind das?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You sort out things left behind after a family visit and notice where speech prefers von plus the dative.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wessen Jacke liegt hier auf dem Sofa?
lines.en: Whose jacket is lying here on the sofa?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das ist die Jacke meiner Schwester.
lines.en: That is my sister's jacket.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und der Schal? Ist das der Schal von deinem Vater?
lines.en: And the scarf? Is that your father's scarf?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, gesprochen sagt man das so. Geschrieben wäre es der Schal meines Vaters.
lines.en: Yes, that is how people say it. In writing it would be der Schal meines Vaters.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nehmen wir die Spielsachen der Kinder mit?
lines.en: Shall we take the children's toys with us?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, aber die Tasche der Nachbarin bleibt hier.
lines.en: Yes, but the neighbour's bag stays here.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und das Ladekabel? Wessen ist das?
lines.en: And the charging cable? Whose is that?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das ist wohl das Kabel meines Onkels. Ich schicke es ihm.
lines.en: That is probably my uncle's cable. I will send it to him.

days.title: Präpositionen mit Genitiv, wegen, trotz und während
days.objective: Give reasons, concessions and time frames with wegen, trotz and während plus the genitive.
days.paretoFocus: These three prepositions carry most written explanations of why something happened, and using them correctly instantly lifts the register of an email or a report.
drills: Write three sentences with wegen explaining why you were late, absent or delayed.
Write two trotz sentences about something you did in spite of bad conditions.
Say four während sentences about what you do during meals, journeys or meetings.

items: Which case do wegen, trotz and während govern in standard written German?
What do many speakers use after wegen in conversation, and why should you avoid it in writing?
Give the masculine, feminine and plural article that follows trotz.

activities.title: Wegen des Wetters
activities.intro: A small group of prepositions governs the genitive, and the three you need most are wegen for a cause, trotz for a concession and während for a time frame. All three take des or der plus the genitive form of the noun. Many speakers say wegen dem Wetter with the dative in conversation, but the standard written form is wegen des Wetters, and that is the form an exam expects from you.

items.hr: Wegen des schlechten Wetters bleiben wir heute zu Hause.
items.en: Because of the bad weather we are staying at home today.
items.note: wegen plus genitive: des Wetters, with the adjective taking -en.

items.hr: Trotz der Verspätung haben wir den Anschluss erreicht.
items.en: In spite of the delay we caught the connection.
items.note: die Verspätung is feminine, so trotz der.

items.hr: Während der Prüfung darf man nicht sprechen.
items.en: During the exam you may not speak.
items.note: während marks a time frame, not a reason.

items.hr: Wegen meines Termins kann ich erst um sechs.
items.en: Because of my appointment I cannot come before six.
items.note: Possessives take the same genitive endings: meines Termins.

items.hr: Viele sagen im Alltag wegen dem Regen, geschrieben heißt es wegen des Regens.
items.en: Many people say wegen dem Regen in everyday life, in writing it is wegen des Regens.
items.note: The dative version is common in speech, the genitive is the written standard.

activities.title: Wegen, trotz oder während?
activities.intro: Practise the genitive after these prepositions and pick the one the meaning needs.

questions.prompt: ___ des Streiks fahren heute keine Busse.
questions.explanation: The strike is the cause of the cancellation, so wegen, and it governs the genitive des Streiks.

questions.prompt: Trotz ___ Kälte sind wir lange spazieren gegangen.
questions.explanation: trotz governs the genitive and die Kälte is feminine, so the feminine genitive article der is used.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct genitive article: Während ___ Ferien war die Stadt angenehm ruhig.
questions.explanation: während governs the genitive and die Ferien is plural, so the plural genitive article is needed.

questions.prompt: Put the words in brackets into the standard written form: Wegen ___ konnte der Zug nicht weiterfahren. (der Sturm)
questions.explanation: In standard written German wegen governs the genitive, even though the dative version is often heard in conversation.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'In spite of the noise I slept well.'
questions.explanation: der Lärm is masculine, so trotz des Lärms, and the prepositional phrase in first position pushes the conjugated verb habe into second position.

activities.title: Alles wegen des Schnees
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague asks why your week went differently than planned and you answer with wegen, trotz and während.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Warum warst du gestern nicht im Büro?
lines.en: Why were you not in the office yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wegen eines Termins beim Arzt. Das war schon lange geplant.
lines.en: Because of an appointment at the doctor's. That had been planned for a long time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und heute Morgen kamen fast alle zu spät.
lines.en: And this morning nearly everyone arrived late.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wegen des Schnees sind die Bahnen viel langsamer gefahren.
lines.en: Because of the snow the trains ran much more slowly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Trotz des Chaos hast du die Präsentation geschafft.
lines.en: In spite of the chaos you managed the presentation.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Während der Fahrt habe ich die letzten Folien fertig gemacht.
lines.en: During the journey I finished the last slides.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Viele sagen ja wegen dem Schnee. Klingt das falsch?
lines.en: Many people do say wegen dem Schnee. Does that sound wrong?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Im Gespräch hört man das oft, aber im Bericht schreibe ich den Genitiv.
lines.en: In conversation you hear that a lot, but in a report I write the genitive.

days.title: Die n-Deklination
days.objective: Recognise the small closed group of masculine nouns that add -n or -en in every case except the nominative singular, and use them correctly.
days.paretoFocus: These nouns name the people you talk about most, colleagues, customers, neighbours and students, so the missing -n is an error that would otherwise show up in almost every conversation.
drills: Write the seven core nouns of this group with their article and say each one in the accusative.
Write four sentences with a dative n-declension noun after mit, bei or von.
Check any text you wrote this week for a masculine noun of this group that is missing its ending.

items: In which single case and number do these nouns have no extra ending?
Name four endings that mark a masculine noun as belonging to this group.
What is the genitive of der Nachbar, and what does it not have that other masculine nouns have?

activities.title: Den Kollegen, dem Studenten
activities.intro: A small closed group of masculine nouns adds -n or -en in every case except the nominative singular. The core list is der Junge, der Kollege, der Kunde, der Mensch, der Nachbar, der Student and der Herr, together with most masculine nouns ending in -ent, -ant, -ist and -oge. Learners who never meet this rule say Ich kenne den Student for years, so it is worth learning the list properly now.

items.hr: Ich kenne den Kollegen schon sehr lange.
items.en: I have known the colleague for a very long time.
items.note: Accusative: der Kollege becomes den Kollegen.

items.hr: Ich habe gestern mit dem Studenten gesprochen.
items.en: I spoke to the student yesterday.
items.note: Dative: dem Studenten, not dem Student.

items.hr: Das ist der Wagen unseres Nachbarn.
items.en: That is our neighbour's car.
items.note: Genitive: des Nachbarn, with -n and without an extra -s.

items.hr: Kannst du bitte dem Herrn dort helfen?
items.en: Can you please help the gentleman over there?
items.note: der Herr takes -n in the singular and -en in the plural: die Herren.

items.hr: Viele Menschen glauben dem Journalisten nicht.
items.en: Many people do not believe the journalist.
items.note: Nouns ending in -ist follow the same pattern: dem Journalisten.

activities.title: Ein Buchstabe mehr
activities.intro: Practise the extra ending on this small group of masculine nouns.

questions.prompt: Ich habe gestern meinen ___ im Supermarkt getroffen.
questions.explanation: der Nachbar belongs to this group, so every case except the nominative singular adds -n, giving den Nachbarn in the accusative.

questions.prompt: Wir haben lange auf den ___ gewartet.
questions.explanation: der Kunde adds -n outside the nominative singular, so the accusative after auf is den Kunden.

questions.prompt: Put the noun in brackets into the correct form: Ich möchte gern mit dem ___ sprechen. (der Praktikant)
questions.explanation: Masculine nouns ending in -ant follow this pattern, so the dative after mit adds -en.

questions.prompt: Put the noun in brackets into the correct form: Das ist das Fahrrad des ___. (der Junge)
questions.explanation: In the genitive these nouns take -n and never the -s that other masculine nouns add.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I have to ask the colleague first.'
questions.explanation: der Kollege is in the accusative here, so it takes -n, and the infinitive fragen closes the sentence after the modal verb.

activities.title: Der neue Kollege
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You talk about people at work and keep the extra ending on every noun of this group.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du den neuen Kollegen schon kennengelernt?
lines.en: Have you met the new colleague yet?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, gestern. Vorher hat er bei einem Kunden von uns gearbeitet.
lines.en: Yes, yesterday. Before that he worked at one of our clients.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was sagt der Chef über den Praktikanten?
lines.en: And what does the boss say about the intern?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er findet ihn gut. Er hilft auch dem Studenten aus dem zweiten Stock.
lines.en: He thinks he is good. He also helps the student on the second floor.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kennst du eigentlich den Namen des Nachbarn im Büro nebenan?
lines.en: Do you actually know the name of the neighbour in the office next door?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das ist Herr Baumann. Frag am besten den Herrn selbst.
lines.en: That is Mr Baumann. It is best to ask the gentleman himself.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Manche Menschen vergessen Namen sofort wieder.
lines.en: Some people forget names again immediately.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich schreibe die Namen der Kunden deshalb immer sofort auf.
lines.en: That is why I always write down the clients' names straight away.

days.title: Der Infinitiv mit zu
days.objective: Build infinitive clauses with zu after verbs, adjectives and nouns, and place zu correctly inside separable verbs.
days.paretoFocus: Expressions like ich habe vergessen, ich habe vor and es ist schwer are the everyday frame for saying what you plan, forget or find difficult.
drills: Write five sentences beginning Ich habe vergessen, Ich habe vor or Ich habe keine Zeit.
Take four separable verbs and write each one as a zu-infinitive, checking that it is one word.
Write three sentences with a modal verb and confirm that none of them contains zu.

items: Where does zu go in a separable verb, and is it written as one word or two?
Name three verbs after which there is no zu at all.
Where in the clause does the infinitive with zu stand?

activities.title: Ich habe vergessen, dich anzurufen
activities.intro: Many verbs, adjectives and nouns are followed by a second verb in the infinitive with zu, which stands at the end of its clause. With a separable verb the zu slips between the prefix and the stem and the whole thing is written as one word: anzurufen, aufzustehen. After modal verbs, and after werden, sehen, hören, lassen and bleiben, there is no zu at all.

items.hr: Ich habe vergessen, dir zu schreiben.
items.en: I forgot to write to you.
items.note: vergessen plus a zu-infinitive, with a comma before the clause.

items.hr: Es ist nicht leicht, jeden Tag früh aufzustehen.
items.en: It is not easy to get up early every day.
items.note: Separable verb: zu goes inside, giving aufzustehen.

items.hr: Wir haben vor, im Sommer nach Hamburg zu fahren.
items.en: We are planning to go to Hamburg in the summer.
items.note: vorhaben is itself separable and takes a zu-infinitive.

items.hr: Ich habe heute keine Lust zu kochen.
items.en: I do not feel like cooking today.
items.note: Nouns such as die Lust and die Zeit also take an infinitive with zu.

items.hr: Ich muss heute leider länger arbeiten.
items.en: Unfortunately I have to work longer today.
items.note: After a modal verb the infinitive stands bare, without zu.

activities.title: Mit zu oder ohne zu?
activities.intro: Practise deciding whether the second verb needs zu and where zu belongs in separable verbs.

questions.prompt: Ich habe gestern versucht, dich ___.
questions.explanation: versuchen takes a zu-infinitive, and in a separable verb zu stands between the prefix and the stem, written as a single word.

questions.prompt: Wir wollen morgen eine Stunde früher ___.
questions.explanation: wollen is a modal verb, so the second verb is a bare infinitive at the end of the sentence without zu.

questions.prompt: Put the verb in brackets into the correct form: Es macht wirklich Spaß, mit euch ___. (kochen)
questions.explanation: Expressions like Spaß machen are followed by an infinitive with zu at the end of the clause.

questions.prompt: Put the verb in brackets into the correct form: Ich habe vergessen, das Fenster ___. (zumachen)
questions.explanation: The prefix here is also zu, so the infinitive marker slips in between and the word becomes zuzumachen.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I have no time to help you today.'
questions.explanation: The noun Zeit takes an infinitive clause, and the infinitive with zu closes it.

activities.title: Pläne für das Wochenende
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend make weekend plans using verbs and expressions that take an infinitive with zu.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du Lust, am Samstag wandern zu gehen?
lines.en: Do you feel like going hiking on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gern, aber ich habe versprochen, meiner Mutter beim Umzug zu helfen.
lines.en: I would love to, but I promised to help my mother with her move.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schade, ich hatte vor, ganz früh loszufahren.
lines.en: What a pity, I was planning to set off really early.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Es ist schwer, alles an einem Tag zu schaffen.
lines.en: It is hard to manage everything in one day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dann versuch doch, am Sonntag mitzukommen.
lines.en: Then try to come along on Sunday.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das kann ich machen. Sonntags muss ich nicht arbeiten.
lines.en: I can do that. On Sundays I do not have to work.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vergiss nicht, feste Schuhe mitzubringen.
lines.en: Do not forget to bring sturdy shoes.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich freue mich darauf, endlich wieder rauszukommen.
lines.en: I am looking forward to finally getting out again.

days.title: um zu und damit
days.objective: Express purpose with um zu or damit, choosing between them according to whether the subject stays the same.
days.paretoFocus: Saying what something is for comes up constantly at work and in explanations, and the same-subject rule is the one point that decides whether the sentence is possible at all.
drills: Write four um zu sentences about things you do for your own benefit.
Write four damit sentences in which the second half has a different subject.
Take two of your um zu sentences and try to rewrite them with damit, noting what has to change.

items: Under which condition is um zu possible, and under which condition is it impossible?
Where does the verb stand in a damit clause?
Why can an um zu clause never contain its own subject?

activities.title: Um zu oder damit
activities.intro: To say what something is for, German uses um plus zu with an infinitive, or the conjunction damit. The choice is grammatical rather than stylistic: um zu is only possible when both halves of the sentence have the same subject, while damit is used when the subject changes. damit introduces a subordinate clause, so its verb goes to the end.

items.hr: Ich lerne jeden Tag, um die Prüfung zu bestehen.
items.en: I study every day in order to pass the exam.
items.note: Same subject in both halves, so um zu is possible.

items.hr: Ich spreche langsam, damit mich alle gut verstehen.
items.en: I speak slowly so that everyone understands me well.
items.note: The subject changes from ich to alle, so damit is required.

items.hr: Er fährt mit dem Rad, um Geld zu sparen.
items.en: He cycles in order to save money.
items.note: um stands at the front of the clause, zu directly before the infinitive.

items.hr: Sie erklärt es noch einmal, damit die Kinder es verstehen.
items.en: She explains it again so that the children understand it.
items.note: The verb verstehen closes the damit clause.

items.hr: Ich stehe früher auf, um pünktlich zu sein.
items.en: I get up earlier in order to be on time.
items.note: An um zu clause never has a subject of its own; it is understood from the main clause.

activities.title: Wozu machst du das?
activities.intro: Decide which form each sentence needs by checking whether the subject changes.

questions.prompt: Ich schreibe alles auf, ___ meine Kollegen später Bescheid wissen.
questions.explanation: The second half has its own subject, meine Kollegen, so an infinitive clause is impossible and damit is required.

questions.prompt: Er nimmt ein Taxi, ___ schneller da zu sein.
questions.explanation: Both halves have the same subject, so the infinitive construction is used: um at the front of the clause and zu before the infinitive at the end.

questions.prompt: Complete with the conjunction that fits, given that the subject changes: Ich rufe dich heute Abend an, ___ du rechtzeitig Bescheid weißt.
questions.explanation: The subject changes from ich to du, so an infinitive clause is impossible and only the conjunction with a full clause works, with its verb at the end.

questions.prompt: Put the verb in brackets into the correct form: Ich fahre in die Stadt, um neue Schuhe ___. (kaufen)
questions.explanation: In this construction the infinitive stands at the very end of the clause with the infinitive marker directly in front of it.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am calling the doctor so that my son gets an appointment.'
questions.explanation: The subject changes from ich to mein Sohn, so damit is used, and the verb bekommt goes to the end of the subordinate clause.

activities.title: Warum machst du das?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about your habits and you explain your reasons with um zu and with damit.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Warum stehst du samstags eigentlich so früh auf?
lines.en: Why do you actually get up so early on Saturdays?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Um in Ruhe zu frühstücken, bevor die anderen wach sind.
lines.en: In order to have breakfast in peace before the others are awake.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und warum machst du abends das Licht im Flur an?
lines.en: And why do you turn on the light in the hall in the evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Damit niemand über die Schuhe fällt.
lines.en: So that nobody trips over the shoes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Du lernst auch spät abends noch. Lohnt sich das?
lines.en: You also study late in the evening. Is that worth it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich lerne jeden Abend, um bald die Prüfung zu machen.
lines.en: Yes, I study every evening in order to take the exam soon.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und warum schreibst du deiner Familie jeden Sonntag?
lines.en: And why do you write to your family every Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Damit sie sich keine Sorgen machen, und um selbst nichts zu verpassen.
lines.en: So that they do not worry, and in order not to miss anything myself.

days.title: ohne zu und anstatt zu
days.objective: Say what did not happen and what happened instead, using ohne zu and anstatt zu, and switch to a full clause when the subject changes.
days.paretoFocus: These two clauses let you narrate and comment with real precision, which is exactly what a spoken or written account of events needs.
drills: Write four ohne zu sentences about things people did without the step you expected.
Write three anstatt zu sentences about a choice you made, putting one of them at the front of the sentence.
Rewrite two of your sentences with ohne dass or anstatt dass by giving the second half its own subject.

items: What does ohne zu tell you about the action in the second half?
Which two forms do you need when the subject in the second half changes?
Where can an anstatt zu clause stand in the sentence?

activities.title: Ohne zu und anstatt zu
activities.intro: Two more infinitive clauses complete the set. ohne plus zu says that something you would expect did not happen, and anstatt plus zu, often shortened to statt, says that one action took the place of another. Like um zu, both need the same subject in both halves; if the subject changes you use ohne dass or anstatt dass with a full clause.

items.hr: Er ist gegangen, ohne sich zu verabschieden.
items.en: He left without saying goodbye.
items.note: The reflexive pronoun sich stands inside the infinitive clause.

items.hr: Sie hat die Prüfung bestanden, ohne viel zu lernen.
items.en: She passed the exam without studying much.
items.note: Same subject in both halves, so the infinitive clause is possible.

items.hr: Anstatt zu telefonieren, schreibt er lieber eine Nachricht.
items.en: Instead of calling, he prefers to write a message.
items.note: The clause can stand first, and then the main verb follows straight after the comma.

items.hr: Ich bin zu Fuß gegangen, anstatt den Bus zu nehmen.
items.en: I walked instead of taking the bus.
items.note: The object den Bus stands inside the clause, before zu nehmen.

items.hr: Er ist gegangen, ohne dass wir es gemerkt haben.
items.en: He left without us noticing it.
items.note: Different subjects, so ohne dass with a full clause and the verb at the end.

activities.title: Was ist nicht passiert?
activities.intro: Practise both infinitive clauses and the switch to a full clause when the subject changes.

questions.prompt: Sie ist losgefahren, ___ vorher zu tanken.
questions.explanation: The expected step, filling up, simply did not happen, so ohne zu is the right clause; anstatt zu would say that she did something else in its place.

questions.prompt: ___ ins Kino zu gehen, sind wir gemütlich zu Hause geblieben.
questions.explanation: One action replaces another, so the clause of replacement is used, and it may stand at the front of the sentence.

questions.prompt: Put the verb in brackets into the correct form: Er hat den Vertrag unterschrieben, ohne ihn vorher ___. (lesen)
questions.explanation: In this infinitive clause the verb stands at the end with the infinitive marker directly in front of it.

questions.prompt: Complete with the two words needed because the subject changes: Er hat alles allein entschieden, ___ ___ wir vorher gefragt wurden.
questions.explanation: The second half has its own subject, wir, so the infinitive clause is impossible and a full clause with its verb at the end is used instead.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'She answered without thinking for long.'
questions.explanation: Both halves share the subject sie, so the infinitive clause works, with zu directly before überlegen at the end.

activities.title: Ohne ein Wort zu sagen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You tell a friend about a strange afternoon, using ohne zu and anstatt zu.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und dann ist er einfach gegangen?
lines.en: And then he simply left?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ohne ein Wort zu sagen.
lines.en: Yes, without saying a word.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du ihn danach noch angerufen?
lines.en: Did you call him afterwards?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, anstatt anzurufen, habe ich ihm lieber geschrieben.
lines.en: No, instead of calling I preferred to write to him.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und hat er geantwortet?
lines.en: And did he answer?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er hat geantwortet, ohne wirklich etwas zu erklären.
lines.en: He answered without really explaining anything.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vielleicht war ihm die ganze Sache peinlich.
lines.en: Maybe he was embarrassed about the whole thing.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Möglich. Anstatt sich zu ärgern, sollte man einfach miteinander reden.
lines.en: Possibly. Instead of getting annoyed, people should simply talk to each other.

days.title: Verben mit festen Präpositionen
days.objective: Use the most common German verbs together with the fixed preposition and case they require, such as warten auf plus accusative and teilnehmen an plus dative.
days.paretoFocus: A German verb and its preposition form one unit that English never predicts, so learning the pair fixes dozens of everyday sentences at once.
drills: Write five sentences about your own week, each using a different verb plus its fixed preposition, and mark the case after each one.
Say aloud the difference between looking forward to something and being pleased about something that already happened, using the same verb both ways.
List every verb from this lesson as verb plus preposition plus case, the same way you would learn a noun with its article.

items: Which case follows warten auf, and which case follows teilnehmen an?
How do you say that you are pleased about something that has already happened?
Name three verbs that take an, and say which case each one governs.

activities.title: Verb plus Präposition
activities.intro: Many German verbs are tied to one fixed preposition, and that preposition also fixes the case of the object. English gives you no help here, because the English verb usually takes a different preposition or none at all. Learn the verb, the preposition and the case together as a single item.

items.hr: Ich warte seit zwanzig Minuten auf den Bus.
items.en: I have been waiting for the bus for twenty minutes.
items.note: warten auf plus Akkusativ: auf den Bus, not auf dem Bus.

items.hr: Ich denke oft an meine Familie in Hamburg.
items.en: I often think about my family in Hamburg.
items.note: denken an plus Akkusativ.

items.hr: Wir freuen uns sehr auf den Urlaub im August.
items.en: We are really looking forward to the holiday in August.
items.note: sich freuen auf plus Akkusativ: something that is still to come.

items.hr: Sie freut sich über das Geschenk von ihren Kollegen.
items.en: She is pleased about the present from her colleagues.
items.note: sich freuen über plus Akkusativ: something that has already happened.

items.hr: Mein Bruder nimmt an einem Sprachkurs teil.
items.en: My brother is taking part in a language course.
items.note: teilnehmen an plus Dativ: an einem Kurs, not an einen Kurs.

activities.title: Welche Präposition passt?
activities.intro: Practise choosing the fixed preposition and the case that each verb demands.

questions.prompt: Ich warte ___ die Antwort meiner Chefin.
questions.explanation: warten takes auf plus the accusative, so die Antwort stays in the accusative form.

questions.prompt: Meine Kollegin nimmt ___ dem Seminar in Köln teil.
questions.explanation: teilnehmen governs an plus the dative, which is why it is dem Seminar and not das Seminar.

questions.prompt: Complete with the missing preposition: 'Ich freue mich schon ___ das Konzert nächsten Samstag.' The concert is still to come.
questions.explanation: For something still ahead of you, German uses sich freuen auf plus the accusative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the missing preposition: 'Sie hat sich sehr ___ die gute Nachricht gefreut.' The news has already arrived.
questions.explanation: For something that has already happened, German uses sich freuen über plus the accusative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'He is thinking about his new job.'
questions.explanation: denken an takes the accusative, so der Job becomes seinen neuen Job.

activities.title: Vor dem Kino
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You meet a friend outside a cinema and every line uses a verb with its fixed preposition.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hallo! Wartest du schon lange auf mich?
lines.en: Hi! Have you been waiting for me long?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich warte erst seit fünf Minuten auf dich.
lines.en: No, I have only been waiting for you for five minutes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Denkst du noch an das Seminar am Samstag?
lines.en: Are you still thinking about the seminar on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich nehme an dem Kurs teil und freue mich schon auf das Wochenende.
lines.en: Yes, I am taking part in the course and I am already looking forward to the weekend.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich habe mich sehr über deine Nachricht gefreut.
lines.en: I was really pleased about your message.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Schön. Dann sprechen wir am Samstag über die Details.
lines.en: Great. Then we will talk about the details on Saturday.

days.title: Präpositionaladverbien, darauf und worauf
days.objective: Replace a thing after a fixed preposition with a da-form such as darauf or damit, and ask about it with a wo-form such as worauf or womit.
days.paretoFocus: Learners who never build these forms sound broken in every second sentence, because German refuses to say auf es or über es for things.
drills: Take five verbs with fixed prepositions and build the matching da-form and wo-form for each.
Write three questions about things and three about people, and notice that only the things use a wo-form.
Say five short answers aloud that begin with a da-form, for example one about a plan and one about a delivery.

items: When do you insert an r between da or wo and the preposition?
How do you ask about a person rather than a thing after a fixed preposition?
Which da-form belongs to teilnehmen an, and which case does that preposition take?

activities.title: Darauf, damit, worauf, womit
activities.intro: When the object of a fixed preposition is a thing, German never says auf es or mit es. Instead it fuses da plus the preposition into one word, and asks with wo plus the preposition. An extra r appears whenever the preposition begins with a vowel, giving darauf, darüber and woran.

items.hr: Worauf wartest du? Ich warte auf die Straßenbahn.
items.en: What are you waiting for? I am waiting for the tram.
items.note: wo plus r plus auf, because auf starts with a vowel. warten auf plus Akkusativ.

items.hr: Die Straßenbahn kommt gleich. Ich warte schon lange darauf.
items.en: The tram is coming soon. I have been waiting for it for a long time.
items.note: darauf replaces auf plus a thing already mentioned.

items.hr: Auf wen wartest du? Auf meinen Bruder.
items.en: Who are you waiting for? For my brother.
items.note: For a person, keep the preposition and use wen or wem, never worauf.

items.hr: Worüber habt ihr so lange gesprochen? Über die neue Miete.
items.en: What did you talk about for so long? About the new rent.
items.note: sprechen über plus Akkusativ, so the wo-form is worüber.

items.hr: Ich freue mich darauf, dich endlich wiederzusehen.
items.en: I am looking forward to seeing you again at last.
items.note: The da-form also points forward to a following zu-clause or dass-clause.

activities.title: Da-Form oder Wo-Form?
activities.intro: Practise building the fused forms for things and keeping the plain preposition for people.

questions.prompt: ___ interessierst du dich? Für moderne Musik.
questions.explanation: sich interessieren takes für plus the accusative, and für begins with a consonant, so no r is inserted.

questions.prompt: Ich nehme an dem Kurs teil. Meine Schwester nimmt auch ___ teil.
questions.explanation: teilnehmen an plus Dativ becomes da plus r plus an, because an begins with a vowel.

questions.prompt: Complete with one word: 'Sie hat mir ihren Plan erklärt, aber ich halte nichts ___.'
questions.explanation: halten von plus Dativ refers back to the plan, a thing, so the fused form is used; von starts with a consonant, so there is no r.

questions.prompt: Complete with one word: 'Ich habe keine Angst ___, allein zu reisen.'
questions.explanation: Angst haben vor plus Dativ, and the fused form is needed because it points forward to the zu-clause.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We are still waiting for it.'
questions.explanation: The thing is replaced by the fused form of auf, which stands at the end of the sentence.

activities.title: Der geplante Umzug
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague asks about your move and you answer with da-forms while she asks with wo-forms.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Worüber habt ihr gestern so lange geredet?
lines.en: What were you talking about for so long yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Über den Umzug. Ich denke die ganze Zeit daran.
lines.en: About the move. I am thinking about it the whole time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Worauf wartest du denn noch?
lines.en: So what are you still waiting for?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Auf die Zusage vom Vermieter. Ich hoffe sehr darauf.
lines.en: For the landlord's approval. I really am hoping for it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wovon hängt seine Entscheidung ab?
lines.en: And what does his decision depend on?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vom Preis. Damit habe ich noch ein kleines Problem.
lines.en: On the price. I still have a small problem with that.

days.title: Das Passiv im Präsens
days.objective: Form and understand the present passive with werden plus the past participle, and name the agent with von or durch when it matters.
days.paretoFocus: Notices, rules, forms and workplace instructions are written almost entirely in the passive, so this unlocks the German that surrounds you every day.
drills: Turn five active sentences about your work or home into the present passive.
Write three notices you have seen in a public building, each one in the passive.
Say three sentences with a named agent, one with von for a person and one with durch for a cause.

items: Which two parts make up the present passive, and where does each part stand?
Which preposition and case introduce a person as the agent?
How do you say that something simply is not done here, without naming anybody?

activities.title: Werden plus Partizip
activities.intro: The German passive is built with werden plus the past participle, and the participle goes to the end of the sentence. The passive puts the action itself in the foreground, so the person doing it often disappears completely. When you do name them, a person takes von plus the dative and a cause or means takes durch plus the accusative.

items.hr: Das Formular wird online ausgefüllt.
items.en: The form is filled in online.
items.note: wird plus Partizip II; das Formular is the subject.

items.hr: Alle Rechnungen werden am Monatsende bezahlt.
items.en: All the invoices are paid at the end of the month.
items.note: Plural subject, so werden.

items.hr: Ich werde jeden Morgen von meinem Nachbarn geweckt.
items.en: I am woken up by my neighbour every morning.
items.note: von plus Dativ for the person responsible.

items.hr: Die Straße wird durch das Hochwasser gesperrt.
items.en: The road is being closed because of the flooding.
items.note: durch plus Akkusativ for a cause or a means.

items.hr: Hier wird nicht geraucht.
items.en: There is no smoking here.
items.note: Impersonal passive with no subject at all, very common on signs.

activities.title: Passiv im Präsens üben
activities.intro: Practise the present passive and the two ways of naming the agent.

questions.prompt: Die Fenster ___ jeden Frühling geputzt.
questions.explanation: Die Fenster is plural, so the passive auxiliary is werden, and the participle stays at the end.

questions.prompt: Das Paket wird ___ meinem Nachbarn angenommen.
questions.explanation: A person acting is introduced with von plus the dative, which is why it is meinem Nachbarn.

questions.prompt: Complete with the past participle of reparieren: 'Mein Fahrrad wird morgen ___.'
questions.explanation: The passive needs the past participle at the end of the sentence.

questions.prompt: Complete with one word: 'In dieser Firma ___ viel Englisch gesprochen.'
questions.explanation: Viel Englisch counts as singular here, so the auxiliary takes the third person singular form.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The rooms are cleaned every day.'
questions.explanation: The auxiliary stands in second position and the participle closes the sentence.

activities.title: Im Bürgerbüro
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You explain to a visitor how applications are handled in your office, using the present passive.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie läuft das hier mit den Anträgen?
lines.en: How does it work here with the applications?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Alle Anträge werden zuerst online ausgefüllt.
lines.en: All applications are filled in online first.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wann werden sie geprüft?
lines.en: And when are they checked?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sie werden meistens innerhalb von zwei Wochen bearbeitet.
lines.en: They are usually processed within two weeks.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wird der Termin dann telefonisch bestätigt?
lines.en: Is the appointment then confirmed by telephone?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, die Antwort wird immer schriftlich geschickt.
lines.en: No, the answer is always sent in writing.

days.title: Das Passiv in der Vergangenheit
days.objective: Tell what was done using the Präteritum passive with wurde and the perfect passive with sein plus the participle plus worden.
days.paretoFocus: Reports, histories and any account of what happened to something rely on the past passive, and the ending worden is the single detail that most learners get wrong.
drills: Describe the history of a building near you in five sentences, all in the Präteritum passive.
Write three sentences about things that have already been done today, using the perfect passive.
Say aloud the difference between ist gebaut worden and ist alt geworden, and explain why the participle changes.

items: Which form of werden carries the Präteritum passive?
How does the perfect passive end, and what would the wrong ending be?
Which auxiliary does the perfect passive use, haben or sein?

activities.title: Wurde und worden
activities.intro: The past passive has two everyday forms. The Präteritum passive uses wurde plus the participle and is the normal choice in writing and in narration. The perfect passive uses sein plus the participle plus worden, and that final worden never becomes geworden.

items.hr: Das Haus wurde 1985 gebaut.
items.en: The house was built in 1985.
items.note: Präteritum passive: wurde plus Partizip II.

items.hr: Die Verträge wurden gestern Nachmittag unterschrieben.
items.en: The contracts were signed yesterday afternoon.
items.note: Plural subject, so wurden.

items.hr: Mein Antrag ist noch nicht bearbeitet worden.
items.en: My application has not been processed yet.
items.note: Perfect passive: sein plus Partizip II plus worden, never geworden.

items.hr: Der Verletzte wurde von einem Passanten gefunden.
items.en: The injured man was found by a passer-by.
items.note: von plus Dativ names the person who did it.

items.hr: Das Konzert ist wegen des Sturms abgesagt worden.
items.en: The concert has been cancelled because of the storm.
items.note: wegen plus Genitiv gives the reason, worden closes the passive.

activities.title: Passiv in der Vergangenheit
activities.intro: Practise choosing between wurde and the perfect passive with worden.

questions.prompt: Die Brücke ___ im Krieg zerstört.
questions.explanation: Die Brücke is singular, so the Präteritum passive uses wurde with the participle at the end.

questions.prompt: Mein Fahrrad ist letzte Woche gestohlen ___.
questions.explanation: In the perfect passive the participle of werden loses its ge, so the sentence ends in worden.

questions.prompt: Complete with the Präteritum passive auxiliary: 'Der Film ___ komplett in Berlin gedreht.'
questions.explanation: One film, one auxiliary in the third person singular Präteritum, with the participle at the end.

questions.prompt: Complete with one word: 'Alle Gäste sind gestern schon informiert ___.'
questions.explanation: The perfect passive always closes with this special participle of werden.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The letter was written by my colleague.'
questions.explanation: von takes the dative, so die Kollegin becomes meiner Kollegin, and the participle stands last.

activities.title: Ein altes Haus
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about the old building you live in, and you answer with past passive forms.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wann wurde dieses Haus eigentlich gebaut?
lines.en: When was this house actually built?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Es wurde 1928 gebaut und vor zehn Jahren komplett renoviert.
lines.en: It was built in 1928 and completely renovated ten years ago.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wurde damals auch das Dach repariert?
lines.en: Was the roof repaired at that time as well?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, das Dach ist letztes Jahr sogar noch einmal erneuert worden.
lines.en: Yes, the roof was even renewed once more last year.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die alten Fenster?
lines.en: And the old windows?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die sind leider noch nicht ausgetauscht worden.
lines.en: Unfortunately they have not been replaced yet.

days.title: Das Passiv mit Modalverben
days.objective: Say what must, can or may be done by combining a modal verb with the participle plus werden at the end of the sentence.
days.paretoFocus: Rules, house regulations and job instructions are almost always phrased as something that must or may be done, so this pattern appears everywhere in daily life.
drills: Write four house rules for your building, each with a modal verb and a passive infinitive.
Turn three sentences with man must into modal passive sentences.
Say three of the same sentences again in the past by changing only the modal verb.

items: In what order do the participle and werden stand at the end of the sentence?
Which word changes when you move a modal passive sentence into the past?
How do you forbid something politely without naming any person?

activities.title: Muss gemacht werden
activities.intro: To say what must or can be done, keep the modal verb in second position and put the participle plus werden at the very end, in that order. Only the modal verb changes for tense, so the past is formed simply with musste or konnte. The infinitive werden never becomes worden in this pattern.

items.hr: Das Formular muss bis Freitag abgegeben werden.
items.en: The form has to be handed in by Friday.
items.note: Modalverb plus Partizip II plus werden at the end.

items.hr: Die Tür kann von außen nicht geöffnet werden.
items.en: The door cannot be opened from outside.
items.note: kann for possibility.

items.hr: Auf diesem Platz darf nicht geparkt werden.
items.en: Parking is not allowed in this square.
items.note: Impersonal, no subject, typical of signs and regulations.

items.hr: Der Termin musste leider verschoben werden.
items.en: The appointment unfortunately had to be postponed.
items.note: Only the modal verb moves into the Präteritum.

items.hr: Diese Frage sollte im Team besprochen werden.
items.en: This question should be discussed in the team.
items.note: sollte for a recommendation.

activities.title: Modalverb plus Passiv
activities.intro: Practise the word order at the end of the sentence and the tense of the modal verb.

questions.prompt: Das Problem muss sofort ___.
questions.explanation: The participle comes first and the infinitive werden closes the sentence; worden belongs only to the perfect passive.

questions.prompt: Welcher Satz ist richtig?
options: Der Motor kann nicht repariert werden.
Der Motor kann nicht werden repariert.
Der Motor kann nicht repariert worden.
Der Motor kann nicht reparieren werden.
questions.answer: Der Motor kann nicht repariert werden.
questions.explanation: The modal stands second, the participle stands next to last and werden stands last.

questions.prompt: Complete with one word: 'Das Zimmer muss vor der Übergabe noch geputzt ___.'
questions.explanation: After the participle the passive infinitive closes the sentence.

questions.prompt: Put the modal verb müssen into the Präteritum: 'Der Flug ___ wegen des Sturms gestrichen werden.'
questions.explanation: In a modal passive only the modal verb carries the tense, so the rest of the sentence stays unchanged.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The window cannot be opened.'
questions.explanation: nicht stands before the verb group, which ends with the participle plus werden.

activities.title: Vor der Wohnungsübergabe
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a flatmate go through everything that still has to be done before you hand the flat back.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was muss vor der Übergabe noch gemacht werden?
lines.en: What still has to be done before the handover?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Wände müssen gestrichen werden und der Boden muss gereinigt werden.
lines.en: The walls have to be painted and the floor has to be cleaned.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kann der Herd hier bleiben?
lines.en: Can the cooker stay here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, er muss leider mitgenommen werden.
lines.en: No, unfortunately it has to be taken with us.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die alte Waschmaschine?
lines.en: And the old washing machine?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die kann nicht mehr repariert werden, sie sollte entsorgt werden.
lines.en: That one cannot be repaired any more, it should be disposed of.

days.title: Alternativen zum Passiv, man und sich lassen
days.objective: Replace a stiff passive with the two structures Germans actually prefer in speech, man plus an active verb and sich lassen for what can be done.
days.paretoFocus: Spoken German avoids the passive far more than textbooks suggest, so these two patterns are what make you sound normal rather than official.
drills: Rewrite four passive sentences with man and say both versions aloud to feel the difference.
Describe three broken things at home and say for each whether it can be fixed, using sich lassen.
Write two sentences with sein plus zu plus infinitive about tasks that still need doing.

items: Which everyday structure means the same as kann repariert werden?
Why does man never appear as an agent after von in a passive sentence?
Give one sentence with man and the same idea again in the passive.

activities.title: Man und sich lassen
activities.intro: German speech often replaces the passive with man plus a normal active verb, which sounds much lighter. A second alternative is sich lassen, which expresses exactly what can be done to something. A third option is sein plus zu plus infinitive, common in written notices.

items.hr: Hier spricht man Deutsch und Türkisch.
items.en: German and Turkish are spoken here.
items.note: Same meaning as hier werden Deutsch und Türkisch gesprochen, but far more natural in speech.

items.hr: In Deutschland trennt man den Müll sehr genau.
items.en: In Germany rubbish is separated very carefully.
items.note: man plus aktives Verb; man is always singular.

items.hr: Der Wasserhahn lässt sich leicht reparieren.
items.en: The tap can be repaired easily.
items.note: sich lassen plus Infinitiv means kann repariert werden.

items.hr: Dieser Fleck lässt sich leider nicht mehr entfernen.
items.en: Unfortunately this stain cannot be removed any more.
items.note: Negative form of the same pattern.

items.hr: Das Problem ist nicht so einfach zu lösen.
items.en: The problem is not so easy to solve.
items.note: sein plus zu plus Infinitiv, a third passive alternative.

activities.title: Ohne Passiv sprechen
activities.intro: Practise swapping a passive sentence for the two spoken alternatives.

questions.prompt: Welcher Satz bedeutet dasselbe wie 'Das kann man leicht ändern'?
questions.explanation: sich lassen is followed by a plain infinitive and expresses possibility, exactly like kann geändert werden.

questions.prompt: In diesem kleinen Restaurant ___ man wirklich gut.
questions.explanation: man always takes the third person singular of the verb, so essen becomes isst.

questions.prompt: Complete with one word: 'Diese alte Uhr ___ sich leider nicht mehr reparieren.'
questions.explanation: The verb has a short vowel before the ss and stands in the third person singular next to sich.

questions.prompt: Rewrite without the passive. 'Hier wird viel gearbeitet.' becomes 'Hier arbeitet ___ viel.'
questions.explanation: The impersonal pronoun takes the subject position and the verb becomes active.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'That can be repaired quickly.'
questions.explanation: The reflexive pronoun follows the verb and the infinitive closes the sentence.

activities.title: Der kaputte Drucker
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague complains about a broken printer and you answer with man and with sich lassen.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Mein Drucker geht seit gestern nicht mehr.
lines.en: My printer has not been working since yesterday.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Zeig mal her. Vielleicht lässt sich das ganz leicht reparieren.
lines.en: Let me have a look. Maybe that can be repaired quite easily.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Meinst du? So etwas kauft man doch heute einfach neu.
lines.en: Do you think so? These days people just buy a new one.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nicht unbedingt. Oft lässt sich das Problem mit einem neuen Kabel lösen.
lines.en: Not necessarily. The problem can often be solved with a new cable.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wenn das nicht hilft?
lines.en: And if that does not help?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann bringt man ihn in eine Werkstatt, das ist schnell erledigt.
lines.en: Then you take it to a repair shop, that is quickly done.

days.title: Konjunktiv II, irreale Wünsche
days.objective: Express wishes about things that are not true, using würde plus infinitive as the default and the surviving simple forms wäre, hätte, könnte and müsste.
days.paretoFocus: Until now this mood only softened requests; used for wishes it lets you talk about the life you do not have, which is where real conversation happens.
drills: Write four wishes about your current situation, two with wäre or hätte and two with würde.
Say three sentences that begin with Ich wünschte and check that the following verb is not in the indicative.
Turn three complaints into wishes with nur or doch, and keep the verb at the end.

items: Which form is the everyday default when a verb has no common simple subjunctive?
Name the subjunctive forms of sein, haben, können and müssen.
Where does the verb stand in a wish that begins with Wenn?

activities.title: Irreale Wünsche
activities.intro: For wishes about things that are not so, German uses the Konjunktiv II. Most verbs simply take würde plus the infinitive, but a small group keeps its own short forms, above all wäre, hätte, könnte and müsste. The little words nur and doch make a wish sound genuinely heartfelt.

items.hr: Ich wäre jetzt gern am Meer.
items.en: I would love to be at the seaside right now.
items.note: sein becomes wäre.

items.hr: Wenn ich nur mehr Zeit hätte!
items.en: If only I had more time!
items.note: haben becomes hätte; in a wish with wenn the verb stands at the end.

items.hr: Ich wünschte, ich könnte besser Deutsch sprechen.
items.en: I wish I could speak German better.
items.note: Ich wünschte introduces an unreal wish; können becomes könnte.

items.hr: Wenn er doch endlich anrufen würde!
items.en: If only he would finally call!
items.note: würde plus Infinitiv is the normal choice for ordinary verbs.

items.hr: Ich wünschte, wir müssten morgen nicht so früh anfangen.
items.en: I wish we did not have to start so early tomorrow.
items.note: müssen becomes müsste; for gehen the older form ginge is still heard.

activities.title: Wenn ich nur ...
activities.intro: Practise the subjunctive forms and the word order of wishes.

questions.prompt: Wenn ich nur ein bisschen mehr Geld ___!
questions.explanation: ich takes hätte; hatte is the ordinary past and states a fact rather than a wish.

questions.prompt: Ich wünschte, ich ___ jetzt im Urlaub.
questions.explanation: After ich wünschte the verb must be in the Konjunktiv II, and sein keeps its simple form.

questions.prompt: Complete with the Konjunktiv II of können: 'Ich wünschte, ich ___ am Wochenende länger schlafen.'
questions.explanation: The modal verb keeps its own subjunctive form with an umlaut instead of taking würde.

questions.prompt: Complete with the everyday subjunctive auxiliary: 'Wenn sie mir doch nur einmal zuhören ___!'
questions.explanation: zuhören has no common simple subjunctive, so the auxiliary carries the mood and stands at the end after the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I wish I had a bigger flat.'
questions.explanation: The second clause is a main clause, so the subjunctive verb stands in second position.

activities.title: Am Ende der Woche
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a colleague are exhausted on a Thursday evening and swap wishes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Du siehst richtig müde aus.
lines.en: You look really tired.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich bin total kaputt. Ich wäre jetzt gern zu Hause.
lines.en: I am completely worn out. I would love to be at home right now.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich auch. Wenn das Wochenende nur schon da wäre!
lines.en: Me too. If only the weekend were here already!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich wünschte, wir müssten morgen nicht um sechs anfangen.
lines.en: I wish we did not have to start at six tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und ich wünschte, ich könnte einfach zwei Wochen verreisen.
lines.en: And I wish I could simply go away for two weeks.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wenn der Chef uns doch endlich mehr Urlaub geben würde!
lines.en: If only the boss would finally give us more holiday!

days.title: Konjunktiv II, irreale Bedingungen mit wenn
days.objective: Build two-part unreal conditions with a wenn-clause and a würde main clause, and give advice with Wenn ich du wäre.
days.paretoFocus: Hypothetical sentences are the backbone of opinions, plans and advice, and this one pattern covers all three.
drills: Write four sentences beginning with Wenn ich and check that the verb stands at the end of that clause.
Take the same four sentences and put the main clause first instead.
Give three pieces of advice to a friend, each one starting with Wenn ich du wäre.

items: Where does the verb stand in the wenn-clause, and where in the main clause?
What happens to the word order if you leave wenn out altogether?
How do you tell someone what you would do in their place?

activities.title: Wenn ich Zeit hätte ...
activities.intro: An unreal condition has two halves. The wenn-clause pushes its subjunctive verb to the end, and the main clause normally uses würde plus the infinitive. If the main clause comes second, it starts with its verb, because the whole wenn-clause already fills the first position.

items.hr: Wenn ich mehr Zeit hätte, würde ich dich öfter besuchen.
items.en: If I had more time, I would visit you more often.
items.note: Verb at the end of the wenn-clause, würde first in the main clause.

items.hr: Wenn das Wetter besser wäre, könnten wir im Garten grillen.
